sources.citation: Campos-Vázquez, R. M., & Vélez-Grajales, R. (2014). Crisis económica y percepciones de movilidad social en México. Sobre México: Temas de Economía, 1(1), 54-78.
sources.relevance: Directly examines perceived social mobility in Mexico, including intergenerational comparisons and beliefs about upward mobility prospects.
sources.key_finding: Economic crises significantly reduce perceived upward mobility, with lower-income Mexicans reporting the sharpest declines in optimism about intergenerational progress.

sources.citation: Torche, F. (2014). Intergenerational mobility and inequality: The Latin American case. Annual Review of Sociology, 40, 619-642.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive framework for understanding both objective and subjective intergenerational mobility in Latin America, including Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Latin America exhibits moderate intergenerational mobility at the middle of the distribution but strong persistence at the extremes, and subjective perceptions often diverge from objective patterns.

sources.citation: Kelley, S. M. C., & Kelley, C. G. E. (2009). Subjective social mobility: Data from 30 nations. In M. Haller, R. Jowell, & T. W. Smith (Eds.), The International Social Survey Programme 1984-2009 (pp. 106-124). Routledge.
sources.relevance: Establishes measurement approaches for subjective social mobility using retrospective and prospective intergenerational comparisons, directly paralleling the construct's question structure.
sources.key_finding: Subjective social mobility is shaped by both actual economic conditions and national cultural narratives about opportunity, with developing countries showing higher perceived upward mobility despite greater inequality.

sources.citation: Vélez-Grajales, R., Campos-Vázquez, R. M., & Huerta-Wong, J. E. (2014). Report on social mobility in Mexico: Imagine your future. Centro de Estudios Espinosa Yglesias (CEEY).
sources.relevance: The foundational Mexican study on social mobility perceptions, combining objective and subjective measures and examining beliefs about whether effort leads to upward mobility.
sources.key_finding: A majority of Mexicans perceive themselves as having experienced upward mobility relative to their parents, but a significant share believe structural barriers prevent young people from achieving similar progress.

sources.citation: Alesina, A., Stantcheva, S., & Teso, E. (2018). Intergenerational mobility and preferences for redistribution. American Economic Review, 108(2), 521-554.
sources.relevance: Demonstrates the causal link between perceived social mobility and policy preferences, providing theoretical grounding for connecting this construct to social policy attitudes.
sources.key_finding: Pessimistic perceptions of social mobility increase support for redistributive policies, and these perceptions are shaped by personal experience, media, and national narratives.

sources.citation: CONEVAL. (2019). Metodología para la medición multidimensional de la pobreza en México (3ra edición). Consejo Nacional de Evaluación de la Política de Desarrollo Social.
sources.relevance: The official Mexican methodology for multidimensional poverty measurement, which includes dwelling quality and asset ownership as core dimensions, directly mirroring this construct.
sources.key_finding: Material deprivation indicators (housing quality, basic services, assets) are essential complements to income-based poverty measures and capture distinct dimensions of well-being in Mexico.

sources.citation: Alkire, S., & Foster, J. (2011). Counting and multidimensional poverty measurement. Journal of Public Economics, 95(7-8), 476-487.
sources.relevance: Provides the foundational methodology (AF method) for constructing composite indices of material deprivation from dwelling and asset indicators, widely adopted in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Multidimensional poverty indices that combine housing quality, asset ownership, and service access provide more policy-relevant poverty identification than income alone.

sources.citation: Filmer, D., & Pritchett, L. H. (2001). Estimating wealth effects without expenditure data—or tears: An application to educational enrollments in states of India. Demography, 38(1), 115-132.
sources.relevance: Establishes the asset-index approach to measuring household socioeconomic status from survey data on dwelling characteristics and durable goods, a method widely used in developing-country surveys.
sources.key_finding: A principal-components-based asset index constructed from housing quality and ownership variables is a robust proxy for long-run household wealth.

sources.citation: McKenzie, D. J. (2005). Measuring inequality with asset indicators. Journal of Population Economics, 18(2), 229-260.
sources.relevance: Evaluates the validity and limitations of asset-based measures of living standards for inequality analysis in Mexico and other developing countries.
sources.key_finding: Asset indices capture durable aspects of living standards well but may underestimate inequality at the top and bottom of the distribution compared to consumption measures.

causal_relationships.related_construct: digital access and information society participation

sources.citation: Feagin, J. R. (1975). Subordinating the poor: Welfare and American beliefs. Prentice-Hall.
sources.relevance: Foundational work establishing the tripartite framework of poverty attributions: individualistic, structural, and fatalistic, which underpins this construct's measurement approach.
sources.key_finding: Americans tend to favor individualistic explanations for poverty, but structural attributions are significantly more common among lower-income and minority respondents.

sources.citation: Rojas-Méndez, R. (2008). Percepciones sobre las causas de la pobreza en México. In R. Cordera & C. J. Cabrera (Eds.), La política social en México: Tendencias y perspectivas. UNAM.
sources.relevance: Directly examines Mexican public attributions for poverty, distinguishing structural from individual explanations in the national context.
sources.key_finding: Mexican respondents who attribute poverty to structural causes (government corruption, lack of opportunities, discrimination) are significantly more likely to support expansive social programs.

sources.citation: Oorschot, W. V., & Halman, L. (2000). The individual and structural causes of poverty: A European comparison. In W. Arts & L. Halman (Eds.), New directions in quantitative comparative sociology (pp. 169-190). Brill.
sources.relevance: Provides cross-national evidence on how structural vs. individual poverty attributions vary by welfare regime and cultural context, offering comparative benchmarks.
sources.key_finding: Structural poverty attributions are more prevalent in countries with higher inequality and weaker welfare states, consistent with the Latin American context.

sources.citation: Merolla, D. M., Hunt, M. O., & Serpe, R. T. (2011). Concentrated disadvantage and beliefs about the causes of poverty: A multi-level analysis. Sociological Perspectives, 54(2), 205-227.
sources.relevance: Demonstrates how neighborhood-level deprivation shapes individual-level structural attributions, relevant to understanding Mexican spatial inequality.
sources.key_finding: Living in areas of concentrated poverty increases structural attributions, net of individual socioeconomic status, suggesting contextual effects on causal beliefs.

causal_relationships.related_construct: social policy preferences and support for redistribution
personal experience of poverty and deprivation
attitudes toward discrimination and human rights

sources.citation: Díaz-Cayeros, A., Estévez, F., & Magaloni, B. (2016). The political logic of poverty relief: Electoral strategies and social policy in Mexico. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes Mexican public preferences for anti-poverty programs in the context of electoral politics, examining how citizens evaluate government effectiveness and program design.
sources.key_finding: Mexican voters' preferences for social policy design are shaped by both programmatic evaluations of effectiveness and clientelistic political relationships, with conditional cash transfers gaining broad support.

sources.citation: Blofield, M., & Martínez Franzoni, J. (2015). Maternalism, co-responsibility, and social equity: A typology of work–family policies. Social Politics, 22(1), 38-59.
sources.relevance: Provides a framework for understanding normative preferences about the role of state vs. family vs. market in social provision, applicable to Mexican policy attitudes.
sources.key_finding: Public preferences for social policy design reflect underlying beliefs about gender roles, state responsibility, and market adequacy, varying systematically across Latin American countries.

sources.citation: Ardanaz, M., & Scartascini, C. (2013). Inequality and personal income tax compliance: Evidence from Latin America. IDB Working Paper No. 390.
sources.relevance: Examines willingness to fund social programs through taxation in Latin America, directly relevant to questions about how anti-poverty policy should be financed.
sources.key_finding: Higher perceived inequality increases stated willingness to pay taxes for social programs, but only when citizens trust that government will use revenues effectively.

sources.citation: Garay, C. (2016). Social policy expansion in Latin America. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the political determinants of social policy expansion in Latin America, providing context for understanding citizen preferences about policy scope and targeting.
sources.key_finding: Public demand for social policy expansion in Latin America is driven by both material need and normative beliefs about state responsibility, with cross-class coalitions supporting universal over targeted approaches.

sources.citation: Zechmeister, E. J., & Corral, M. (2013). Individual and contextual constraints on ideological labels in Latin America. Comparative Political Studies, 46(6), 675-701.
sources.relevance: Demonstrates how ideological self-placement relates to policy preferences in Latin America, relevant to understanding the ideological structure of social policy attitudes in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Ideological labels in Latin America map onto social policy preferences, but the mapping is weaker and more context-dependent than in advanced democracies.

causal_relationships.related_construct: gender role attitudes and women's economic participation

sources.citation: Rojas, M. (2008). Experienced poverty and income poverty in Mexico: A subjective well-being approach. World Development, 36(6), 1078-1093.
sources.relevance: Directly examines subjective economic well-being in Mexico, demonstrating that self-assessed economic satisfaction captures dimensions of poverty missed by income measures.
sources.key_finding: Subjective economic well-being in Mexico is only moderately correlated with income, and factors such as social comparison, perceived control, and family relationships independently predict life satisfaction among the poor.

sources.citation: Graham, C., & Pettinato, S. (2002). Frustrated achievers: Winners, losers, and subjective well-being in new market economies. Journal of Development Studies, 38(4), 100-140.
sources.relevance: Introduces the concept of 'frustrated achievers' — people whose objective conditions improve but subjective well-being declines — highly relevant to understanding the disconnect between material and subjective measures.
sources.key_finding: In Latin America, rapid economic change creates a gap between objective mobility and subjective satisfaction, with relative deprivation and rising aspirations undermining well-being gains.

sources.citation: Lora, E. (Ed.). (2008). Beyond facts: Understanding quality of life. Inter-American Development Bank.
sources.relevance: Comprehensive IDB study of subjective well-being in Latin America covering satisfaction with work, health, leisure, and economic situation — dimensions directly measured in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Latin Americans report higher subjective well-being than predicted by their income levels, but domain-specific satisfaction (work, economic situation) is more closely tied to objective conditions than overall life satisfaction.

sources.citation: Diener, E., Oishi, S., & Lucas, R. E. (2003). Personality, culture, and subjective well-being: Emotional and cognitive evaluations of life. Annual Review of Psychology, 54, 403-425.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical framework for measuring subjective well-being through multiple domains (happiness, life satisfaction, domain satisfaction, perceived control), which this construct operationalizes.
sources.key_finding: Subjective well-being is a multidimensional construct requiring measurement of both cognitive evaluations (life satisfaction, domain satisfaction) and affective components (happiness), as these tap distinct psychological processes.

sources.citation: Palomar Lever, J. (2005). Percepción de las causas de la pobreza, factores psicológicos asociados y percepción de la movilidad social. In M. Székely (Ed.), Desmitificando la pobreza en México. PNUD/SEDESOL.
sources.relevance: Examines psychological correlates of poverty in Mexico, including locus of control, self-efficacy, and subjective class identification — dimensions captured in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Internal locus of control and self-efficacy are significantly associated with both subjective well-being and actual economic outcomes among poor Mexicans, suggesting psychological resources mediate the poverty-well-being link.

causal_relationships.related_construct: material living standards and objective poverty
employment quality and labor market conditions
